flashcards.front: What is a Minimum Cost Spanning Tree (MST)?
flashcards.back: A spanning tree of a weighted connected graph whose total edge weight is minimum among all possible spanning trees. It has V-1 edges, connects all vertices, and is acyclic.
1) Sort all edges by weight. 2) Pick the smallest edge. 3) If it does not form a cycle (check using Union-Find), add it to MST. 4) Repeat until V-1 edges are added. Time: O(E log E).

1) Start from any vertex, add it to MST set. 2) Among all edges connecting MST vertices to non-MST vertices, pick the one with minimum weight. 3) Add the chosen vertex and edge to MST. 4) Repeat until all vertices are included. Time: O(E log V) with heap, O(V^2) with matrix.

flashcards.front: When is Kruskal's preferred over Prim's?
flashcards.back: Kruskal's is preferred for sparse graphs (E is small relative to V^2) because its complexity depends on E. Prim's with adjacency matrix is preferred for dense graphs (O(V^2) is better when E is close to V^2).

flashcards.front: What is the cut property of MSTs?
flashcards.back: For any cut (partition of vertices into two sets) of the graph, the minimum weight edge crossing the cut must be in every MST. This property is what makes greedy MST algorithms correct.
If all edge weights are distinct, the MST is unique. If some edge weights are equal, there may be multiple MSTs with the same total weight.
